system: AR-VIEW — the AR encounter/catch screen for rapp-go. A full-bleed rear-camera backdrop with the already-grown .egg creature composited "into the world" as a softly glowing organism, DeviceOrientation to turn-and-find it, and a flick-to-throw orb. It is a self-contained overlay (no map, no network of its own) that receives one already-grown creature cart, runs the throw gesture, delegates catch RNG to the CATCH system, and on success drops a real .egg into the shared basket → companion/cabinet/lantern loop. Graceful degradation: rear-cam-only (no orientation) → centered encounter; no-camera → a framed birth-void encounter that is also the desktop/reduced-motion default.
design:
SCREEN (z-stack, all in rapp-go/index.html, driven by rapp-go/ar-view.js):
1. `<video id="ar-cam" playsinline autoplay muted>` — rear camera (facingMode:'environment'), object-fit:cover, full-bleed. Display-only; frames never leave the device.
2. `#ar-atmosphere` — a near-invisible vignette + faint cool tint keyed to the creature's birth sky (reuse voidTint) so the creature reads as belonging to the scene, not pasted on. Very low opacity.
3. `<iframe id="ar-creature" src="player.html?bg=world#<b64url cart>">` — the EXISTING 3D player in a new transparent AR mode; pointer-events:none. Positioned + scaled by orientation to sit ~2.5 m out at ~40% viewport height. Keeps its halo/glow (that IS the "glowing softly in the real world").
4. `<canvas id="ar-fx">` — pointer-events:auto; the swipe-capture surface AND the render layer for the thrown orb, its trail, the focus reticle, and the timing ring.
5. `#ar-hud` — deliberately sparse: top-center a single line "creature name · place"; bottom a translucent orb-tier chip (tap to cycle tier, long-press to apply a berry/aid); a one-time fading "flick up to throw" hint; an edge chevron only when the creature is off-view; a small ✕. No health bars, no counters, no arcade.
6. `#ar-result` — on resolve: "Kept ✓" with three quiet doors (Talk / Breed / Keep) or "it broke free" / "it slipped away."

FIND-IT (world anchoring): on entry pick an anchor bearing θ0 = current compass heading offset by ±25–40° (so it starts just off-frame, inviting you to turn) and a small elevation φ0 near the horizon. Each orientation event → Δaz = shortestAngle(heading, θ0), Δel = pitch − φ0. Screen x = cx + Δaz·pxPerDeg, y = cy − Δel·pxPerDeg with pxPerDeg = viewportW / ~60° FOV. Off-view → hide iframe, show an edge chevron ("turn ←"). When |Δaz| < ~12° it "locks": reticle tightens, a soft chime, throwing enabled. A subtle apparent-size parallax (scale 0.9–1.1) sells depth.

THROW GESTURE (this is what AR-VIEW owns and emits): pointerdown on #ar-fx begins a tracked path; pointerup computes release velocity from the last ~80 ms, straightness, and aim vector. A timing ring loops large→small over the locked creature; release at min radius = bonus (nice/great/excellent). AR-VIEW spawns an orb sprite (soft translucent radial-gradient sphere in the tier's color, faint trail) that arcs on a parabola toward the aim point while shrinking to fake distance. On reaching the creature plane it emits a single throw event: {orbTier, aidActive, aimHit, quality 0..1, ringBonus 0..1, hitRadius}. AR-VIEW does NOT decide catch — it calls the CATCH resolver, then animates the returned outcome: N wobbles (orb pulses) → burst + keep, or the creature shivers free (break), or after the encounter timer expires it "slips away."

FALLBACK MATRIX: (a) camera + orientation → full AR above. (b) camera, no/denied orientation or desktop → same but creature centered, gently auto-orbiting (player's own idle orbit), iframe pointer-events re-enabled so you can drag to look; skip find-it. (c) no camera / reduced-motion / desktop default → "framed encounter": the player in its NORMAL opaque birth-void mode inside a rounded card on a dim backdrop; identical flick-to-throw; no permissions needed. All three share one throw/catch/keep code path.
build:
STATIC, no build step, no CDN. Only "network" AR-VIEW touches is loading the local player.html iframe — the cart is already grown by the ENCOUNTER system, so AR-VIEW works fully offline.

FILES:
- rapp-go/player.html — vendored copy of hologram/player.html (as companion/ already does) plus one AR mode gated on `?bg=world`: (1) read the param at boot; set module flag TRANSPARENT; (2) in renderScene() wrap the background fill (hologram/player.html:573-574 `ctx.fillStyle=bg; ctx.fillRect(...)`) in `if(!TRANSPARENT)` so the canvas stays cleared/transparent; (3) skip drawShadow() in world mode (no floor to cast on) but KEEP drawHalo() so the glow survives; (4) CSS: `body.ar,#stage.ar,canvas.ar{background:transparent!important}` and hide #panel/#orbit-hint/#verify chrome; set the iframe element itself background:transparent. Optional enhancement: add `window.addEventListener('message', e=>{ if(e.data?.type==='ar-yaw'){ VIEW.autoOrbit=0; VIEW.yaw=e.data.yaw; } })` so body rotation can subtly orbit the creature (MVP omits this; translation alone is enough).
- rapp-go/ar-view.js — ES module (hand-rolled, no deps). Exports `openEncounter(encounter, {onCaught})`. Owns: getUserMedia rear-cam start/stop (mirrors companion/index.html:507-508,543 but facingMode:'environment'), DeviceOrientation listener + iOS `DeviceOrientationEvent.requestPermission()` + `webkitCompassHeading` handling, the #ar-fx requestAnimationFrame loop (orb physics, ring, reticle), the pointer throw tracker, the fallback matrix, and the result doors. Uses b64url helper copied from companion/index.html:138-140 to build the iframe src.
- rapp-go/index.html — adds the #ar overlay markup + CSS and imports ar-view.js; the MAP/ENCOUNTER code calls openEncounter() when the player taps a spawn.

CAMERA: `getUserMedia({video:{facingMode:{ideal:'environment'}},audio:false})` → video.srcObject; on exit `stream.getTracks().forEach(t=>t.stop())`. HTTPS satisfied by GitHub Pages. Both getUserMedia and orientation requestPermission are triggered from the single "Look for it →" tap (iOS gesture requirement).
ORIENTATION: feature-detect `deviceorientationabsolute` → else `deviceorientation`+`webkitCompassHeading` (iOS) → else relative alpha drift → else fallback (b). Low-pass filter headings.
ORB/RING/RETICLE: plain 2D canvas draws (radial gradients, arcs) — cheap. Orb parabola + shrink = fake z. Generous hitRadius scaled by ringBonus and rarity for aim-assist.
PERF/BATTERY: cap #ar-creature CSS size (~360–420 px, not full screen), cap player DPR to 2, throttle AR to 30fps, pause on `visibilitychange`, hard-stop all tracks + RAF on exit, timebox the encounter (~90 s) then "slipped away." Honor prefers-reduced-motion → framed mode.
integration:
FITS THE .egg + basket/cabinet/companion loop exactly, touching no existing schema.
- INPUT contract (ENCOUNTER → AR-VIEW): `openEncounter({ cart, rarity:0..1, spawnId, place:{name,geohash,coord}, expiresAt }, {onCaught})`. `cart` is a finished `hologram-cartridge/1.0` egg already grown by rapp-go from the spawn's real coords+live weather+time via the SHARED `momentToGenome()`+`genomeId()` pipeline (hologram/index.html:1020,708-719) — same deterministic sha256(genome) id, same `born:{coord: geohash·epoch, from:"live T°C · code W · wind · day/night"}`. AR-VIEW renders it as-is; it invents no genome. So a caught creature is byte-identical to a cabinet/companion egg and carries its real place+moment.
- CATCH contract (AR-VIEW → CATCH system): AR-VIEW emits the throw event and awaits `resolveCatch({rarity, orbTier, aidActive, quality, ringBonus})→{outcome:'caught'|'break'|'flee', wobbles}`; AR-VIEW only animates. Inventory spend (orb consumed) is CATCH/INVENTORY's job.
- SUCCESS (AR-VIEW → BASKET): on 'caught' call `keepToBasket(cart)` copied verbatim from companion/index.html:551-556 → shared `indexedDB 'rapp-basket'` store `eggs` keyPath id, record `{id,egg:cart,title,born,addedAt}`. From there it already flows to cabinet/companion/lantern with zero extra work.
- THREE DOORS in #ar-result reuse companion's deep-links (companion/index.html:549-559): Talk → COMPANION `?token=<b64url cart>` (its whole self is this place's birth moment); Breed → CABINET `#remix=<b64url cart>` into the one gene pool + lineage tree; Keep → basket (already written). 
- OPTIONAL keepsake postcard: on catch, composite the current video frame + the transparent creature canvas into one still (canvas drawImage) → offer as a downloadable "postcard of this place" and/or attach to the egg's existing keepsake-note field (companion cycle-8 keepsake) so the creature can later "remember where you met." Only created on opt-in; no frames stored otherwise.
ontaste: The soul is "you caught a real moment of a real place you walked to," and AR-VIEW's whole job is to make that quiet, not loud. The creature is the SAME organism deterministically grown from that spot's live sky — in AR it simply stands in the actual air in front of you, lit by its birth-sky light rig and wearing only its own halo/glow (we deliberately drop the floor shadow and any fake occlusion — no plastic AR gimmick, just a soft luminous presence). The HUD is one line of text and one orb chip; no health bars, damage numbers, combo meters, or confetti. "Turn to find it" is a calm act of looking around a place, not a radar minigame. The throw is a single gentle flick with a soft chime, and success is a quiet "Kept ✓" that hands you straight to talking to it, not a jackpot animation. The birth-sky tint bleeds faintly into the camera vignette so the creature belongs to the scene. The honest framed-fallback (creature in its own birth void, no camera) is treated as a first-class, beautiful mode — not a punishment — reinforcing that the point is the moment, not the spectacle. Camera is display-only and never uploaded, stated plainly.
risks: getUserMedia + DeviceOrientation need HTTPS and a user gesture; iOS also needs DeviceOrientationEvent.requestPermission()/webkitCompassHeading. Mitigation: one gesture-gated 'Look for it →' button requests camera then orientation; robust fallbacks if either is denied.

Compass heading is inconsistent — deviceorientationabsolute is patchy on Android, iOS exposes webkitCompassHeading on deviceorientation. Mitigation: feature-detect and degrade absolute→relative-drift→centered no-anchor mode; low-pass filter to kill jitter.

Software-3D canvas over a live video feed is CPU/GPU/battery heavy and can overheat phones. Mitigation: cap creature iframe CSS size + DPR≤2, throttle to 30fps, timebox encounters ~90s, pause on visibilitychange, hard-stop tracks+RAF on exit.

iframes render opaque by default in some engines, breaking compositing. Mitigation: the ?bg=world player sets body/canvas/#stage background:transparent and the iframe element itself transparent; verify the bg fillRect is actually skipped.
Camera conflict — companion or another surface holding getUserMedia can black out the feed. Mitigation: single active stream, always stop tracks on exit; retry-once on 'device in use'.
Aiming at a small distant creature is fiddly and frustrating. Mitigation: aim-assist — generous hitRadius scaled by ring-timing bonus and rarity, plus a lock zone when |Δaz|<12°.
Motion parallax can cause discomfort / hits reduced-motion users. Mitigation: honor prefers-reduced-motion → framed static mode, disable parallax and world-anchor.
Privacy perception of a rear camera pointed at people/streets. Mitigation: explicit 'stays on your device, never uploaded' note; no frames persisted unless the user opts into a keepsake postcard.

No real occlusion/lighting means naive compositing can look pasted-on and gimmicky. Mitigation: lean on the birth-sky light rig + halo + shared vignette tint and drop the fake floor shadow, so it reads as a soft glowing presence rather than a floating sticker.
mvp: Smallest genuinely-playable AR-VIEW: `openEncounter(cart)` opens a full-screen overlay with (1) rear-camera `<video>` backdrop, (2) the vendored `player.html?bg=world#<b64url cart>` transparent iframe centered at ~40% height with its halo showing over the real world (gentle idle auto-orbit — NO orientation yet), (3) a #ar-fx canvas that captures a pointer flick, spawns one orb sprite that arcs+shrinks toward the aim, and if the aim overlaps the creature calls a stub `resolveCatch()` (fixed 70% until CATCH lands) → on success runs the verbatim companion `keepToBasket(cart)` and shows 'Kept ✓' with a Talk door (companion ?token=) — proving the real egg lands in the basket and flows onward. Fallback already free: if getUserMedia fails, the same overlay renders the creature in normal opaque birth-void mode (framed encounter) with the identical throw. That is a real, offline, catch-a-real-moment loop on day one. THEN layer in, in order: DeviceOrientation turn-to-find + edge chevrons; the timing ring + nice/great/excellent bonus; orb-tier chip + berry/aid; and the real CATCH resolver with wobble/break/flee animations and the keepsake postcard.
